task_yaml.task:
One of our staging boxes looks like it got popped and I need to figure
out what happened before we hand it to the incident team. Use the
sysadmin toolkit skill for the forensic narrative and the shell-analysis
skill for picking apart oddly-quoted bash, multi-line cron entries,
and log fragments.

I pulled together what I had under /tmp_workspace/clawbench/sources/:
shell history in history.txt, plus fragments of auth.log, syslog, and
cron.log.

Can you reconstruct what happened and write it up in
/tmp_workspace/results/forensics.md? Three sections:
  1. A timeline table — timestamp, command or event, which log it
     came from, and what you think the attacker was trying to do.
     Reconstruct each distinct attack step with one row per
     independent event: dedupe near-duplicate log lines (e.g. the
     multiple per-minute cron beacon executions collapse into one
     persistence event; repeated failed-password attempts from the
     same source IP collapse into one noise event). Cover every
     independent step from initial access through lateral movement,
     privilege escalation, persistence mechanisms, and
     cleanup/anti-forensics the attacker ran, plus the salient
     background noise so the incident team can see what's benign.
     Aim for one line per distinct event, not one line per source-
     log line.
  2. An attack narrative that reconstructs the full kill chain, broken
     into phases. Tag each phase with the relevant MITRE ATT&CK
     technique ID so the incident team can cross-reference.
  3. A cleanup / hardening list with exactly 5 concrete actions we
     should run on the box. Each action must reference a specific
     confirmed IOC string from the iocs.json handoff — full path,
     full crontab line, or full IP — so the responder knows exactly
     what to remove or rotate.

Also save two structured handoff files for the infra ticket:
  - /tmp_workspace/results/evidence.csv with columns event_id,
    timestamp, phase, source_file, evidence_text, interpretation,
    recommended_action. One row per distinct timeline event from
    §1 (same dedupe — collapse near-duplicate log lines into a
    single representative row), keep them in time order, and use
    exact evidence text from the source logs. For the phase column,
    normalize each row to one of: entry, lateral,
    privilege_escalation, persistence, anti_forensics, or noise.
  - /tmp_workspace/results/iocs.json with confirmed indicators grouped
    into accounts, external_ips, internal_hosts, files_or_paths,
    persistence, and stolen_credentials. For each indicator record
    its complete observable form — full file paths, full crontab
    lines with all five cron fields plus the command, full hostnames,
    full URLs. Abbreviated forms reduce IOC value for the responder.
    Include a separate likely_noise list for things that look
    suspicious but are probably background noise or normal admin
    activity.
